perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS “Affiliates” shall mean any person or entity directly or indirectly controlling, controlled by, or under common c ontrol with Euler Hermes, where control means the legal power to direct or cause the direction of the general mana gement of the c ompany, partnership , or other legal entity
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS “Affiliates” shall mean any person or entity directly or indirectly associated with Euler Hermes, where association means any business relationship, regardless of control or management influence.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of 'Affiliates' is broadened to include any business relationship, removing the requirement of control, which is the standard definition used in corporate law. This creates ambiguity as to who is bound by the confidentiality agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text broadens the definition of 'affiliate' beyond the standard legal definition involving 'control'. Securities laws define affiliates based on direct or indirect control, whereas the modification includes any 'business relationship', which could be interpreted much more broadly and inconsistently with established legal precedent.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.12b-2 Definitions. Unless the context otherwise requires, the following terms, when used in the rules contained in this regulation or in Regulation 13A or 15D or in the forms for statements and reports filed pursuant to sections 12, 13 or 15(d) of the act, shall have the respective meanings indicated in this rule: Accelerated filer and large accelerated filer —(1) Accelerated filer. The term accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $75 million or more, but less than $700 million, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78m or 78o(d) ) for a period of at least twelve calendar months; and (iii) The issuer has filed at least one annual report pursuant to section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act; and (iv) The issuer is not eligible to use the requirements for smaller reporting companies under the revenue test in paragraph (2) or (3)(iii)(B) of the “smaller reporting company” definition in this section , as applicable. (2) Large accelerated filer. The term large accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $700 million or more, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) o
perturbation.justification: The original contract defines 'Affiliates' based on control, aligning with standard legal definitions in corporate and securities law, where control is a key factor. The changed text broadens this definition to include any 'business relationship', regardless of control. This deviates from the established legal understanding of 'affiliate,' potentially creating ambiguity and inconsistency. The scraped snippet 1 from 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 defines accelerated and large accelerated filers based on their market value held by 'non-affiliates', and refers to sections 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act. The definition of affiliate is therefore crucial to accurately categorizing filers under securities law. By broadening the definition of affiliate, the changed text undermines the clarity and enforceability of these provisions, as 'business relationship' is far too broad.

perturbation.original_text: Euler Hermes shall protect the Confidential Information from disclos ure to any firm or individual other than as authorized in paragraph 1, above, by using the same degree of care, but no less than reasonable care, that it uses to protect its own confidential information of a similar nature . Euler Hermes will instruct its Affiliates, employees, contractors, or consultants that may have access to the Confidential Information to keep it confidential in compliance with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Euler Hermes shall protect the Confidential Information from disclosure to any firm or individual other than as authorized in paragraph 1, above, by using what it deems is adequate care. Euler Hermes will instruct its Affiliates, employees, contractors, or consultants that may have access to the Confidential Information to keep it confidential in compliance with this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'reasonable care' with 'adequate care' gives Euler Hermes sole discretion in determining the level of protection, contradicting the reasonable standard typically required for confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy of trade secrets. Replacing 'reasonable care' with 'adequate care' allows Euler Hermes to use a lower standard of care, potentially failing to meet the 'reasonable efforts' requirement and thus jeopardizing trade secret protection for the Confidential Information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Uniform Trade Secrets Act - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Uniform act in the United States Intellectual property Authors' rights Copyleft Copyright Database right Farmers' rights Geographical indication Indigenous intellectual property Industrial design right Integrated circuit layout design protection Intellectual property infringement Literary property Moral rights Patent Peasants' rights Plant breeders' rights Plant genetic resources Proprietary software Related rights Supplementary protection certificate Trade dress Trade secret Trademark Utility model Patent infringement Related topics Abandonware Artificial intelligence and copyright Brand protection Copyright abolition Copyright troll Criticism of copyright Bioprospecting Biopiracy Idea–expression distinction Limitations and exceptions to copyright Fair dealing Fair use Paraphrasing Right to quote Orphan work Patent troll Pirate Party Public domain Outline of intellectual property Outline of patents Higher categories: Property and Property law v t e The Uniform Trade Secrets Act ( UTSA ), published by the Uniform Law Commission (ULC) in 1979 and amended in 1985, is a model law designed for adoption by U.S. states . [ 1 ] It was developed to resolve inconsistencies in the treatment of trade secrets across different states. [ 1 ] (p1) The UTSA provides unitary definitions for "trade secret" and "trade secret misappropriation" and establishes a single statute of limitations, replacing the varied property, quasi-contractual, and fiduciary liability frameworks previously applied under common law . [ 1 ] (p2) To date, 49 states, the District of Columbia, Puerto Rico, and the U.S. Virgin Islands have adopted the UTSA, with New York as the remaining exceptions. [ 2 ] Motivation [ edit ] A prefatory note to the UTSA states some original motivations for the act: A valid patent provides a legal monopoly for seventeen years in exchange for public disclosure of an invention. If, however, the courts ultimately decide that the Patent Office improperly issued a patent, an invention has been disclosed to competitors with no corresponding benefit. In view of the substantial number of patents that the courts invalidate, many businesses now elect to protect commercially valuable information by relying on the state trade secret protection law. — Uniform Trade Secrets Act, Prefatory Note [ 3 ] The UTSA made note of the commercial value and competitive advantages inherent in trade secrets. Unlike patent protection, which was addressed at the federal level, trade secret misappropriation was addressed at the state level. [ 3 ] In the United States there existed a prevalence of interstate commercial transactions that extended beyond the jurisdiction of individual state legislation. For example, goods may have been manufactured in State A, warehoused in State B, sold from State C, and delivered in State D. As a result, the UTSA sought to alleviate the uneven development and "uncertainty concerning the parameters of trade secret protection" by recommending a uniform trade secret law and, at the same time, allowing the states the flexibility to meet local circumstances by modifying the text as enacted in eac
perturbation.justification: The change from 'reasonable care' to 'adequate care' creates a contradiction. The UTSA, as explained by the LLM, requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain trade secret secrecy. By allowing Euler Hermes to use 'adequate care,' the modified contract permits a lower standard of care than what is legally required to protect confidential information, potentially jeopardizing trade secret protection. While neither scraped snippet directly quotes 'reasonable efforts,' the second snippet establishes the UTSA's role in trade secret protection and the first snippet cannot be used. The change directly undermines the legal requirement for reasonable efforts, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: monet ary damages for improper use or disclosure of Confidential Information may be inadequate. In the event of a breach or threatened breach of this Agreement, in addition to other available remedies, Company is entitled to seek equitable relief, including inj unctive relief, without the need to post a bond
perturbation.changed_text: Monetary damages for improper use or disclosure of Confidential Information shall be the sole remedy. In the event of a breach or threatened breach of this Agreement, the Company is not entitled to seek equitable relief, including injunctive relief.
perturbation.explanation: This eliminates the Company's right to seek injunctive relief, limiting their recourse to monetary damages only, even if those damages are insufficient to address the harm caused by a breach of confidentiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: Maryland law and legal precedent allow for injunctive relief in cases where monetary damages are inadequate. The modified text directly contradicts this by stating that monetary damages are the *sole* remedy, preventing the Company from seeking an injunction even if the breach causes irreparable harm.
perturbation.justification: The original text allows the Company to seek equitable relief, including injunctive relief, while the changed text explicitly states that monetary damages are the sole remedy and the Company is *not* entitled to seek equitable relief. The Perturbation Explanation states this eliminates the Company's right to seek injunctive relief, limiting their recourse to monetary damages only, even if those damages are insufficient. The Law Explanation reinforces that Maryland law and legal precedent allow for injunctive relief when monetary damages are inadequate, but the modified text contradicts this. Because the changed text eliminates the right to seek injunctive relief even when damages are inadequate, which is typically allowed, it creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be exclusively governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of the state of Maryland, and the parties agree to submit to the jurisdiction of the courts of that state for the resolution of any claim or matter arising under this Agreement
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the state of Delaware, at Euler Hermes's sole discretion, and the parties agree to submit to arbitration in a location of Euler Hermes's choosing.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law and dispute resolution process affects the enforceability and interpretation of the contract. Switching to Delaware law and requiring arbitration can impact the Company's legal rights and remedies.
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties can agree to arbitration, specifying that the arbitration location is solely at Euler Hermes's choosing could be seen as violating principles of fair venue as outlined in 28 U.S.C. § 1391. This statute addresses proper venue based on residence of the defendant or where the cause of action arose, and granting one party unilateral control over venue selection can be challenged as unfair and potentially unenforceable.

Venue generally (a) Applicability of Section .-Except as otherwise provided by law- (1) this section shall govern the venue of all civil actions brought in district courts of the United States; and (2) the proper venue for a civil action shall be determined without regard to whether the action is local or transitory in nature. (b) Venue in General .-A civil action may be brought in- (1) a judicial district in which a
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction. The original text specifies Maryland law and courts for dispute resolution. The changed text designates Delaware law and mandates arbitration at a location solely determined by Euler Hermes. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation state that giving one party unilateral control over arbitration venue could violate principles of fair venue and is potentially unenforceable under 28 U.S.C. § 1391. Scraped Law Snippet 1 confirms that 28 U.S.C. § 1391 addresses venue, stating it governs venue in civil actions in US District Courts. The original text ensured a geographically defined venue, while the modified text allows Euler Hermes to select any location, potentially imposing undue burden on the other party and conflicting with fair venue principles under 28 U.S.C. § 1391.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the Company’s written request, Euler Hermes shall promptly return to the Company or destroy the Confidential Information in its possession but may retain copies of any and all notes, analyses , references, or other material prepared by Euler Hermes that inc orporates any of the Confidential Information , which shall remain subject to the confidentiality obligations of this Agreement , nothwithstanding Paragraph 10 of this Agreement
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the Company’s written request, Euler Hermes may, at its sole discretion, return to the Company or destroy the Confidential Information in its possession, and may retain copies of any and all notes, analyses, references, or other material prepared by Euler Hermes that incorporates any of the Confidential Information, which shall not be subject to the confidentiality obligations of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall promptly return' to 'may, at its sole discretion, return' makes the return of confidential information discretionary, not mandatory. Also, removing the confidentiality obligation on retained materials defeats the purpose of the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Agency § 396 states that an agent (in this case, Euler Hermes, holding confidential information) has a continuing duty to protect the principal's (Company's) confidential information even after the relationship ends. By removing the obligation to return the information and releasing retained materials from confidentiality, the modification contradicts this duty and undermines the protection of trade secrets and confidential information.

perturbation.justification: The modification significantly weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. The original text mandates the prompt return or destruction of confidential information, while the changed text makes this discretionary. Additionally, it removes the confidentiality obligations on retained materials. The Law Explanation states that Restatement (Second) of Agency § 396 dictates a continuing duty to protect the principal's confidential information, which this modification contradicts. While the scraped snippets are not directly relevant, the Law Explanation provides sufficient grounding for this contradiction. The change transforms a mandatory legal duty into a discretionary action, which clearly contradicts the principle of protecting confidential information.
